result:
There is a peculiar beauty in the way the morning light catches the dust motes dancing in a sunbeam. Most of the time, we move through our days with blinkered vision, focused entirely on the destination—the next meeting, the unfinished task, the lingering to-do list. We treat our lives like a commute, something to be endured until we finally arrive at a point of rest. But if you stop for even a second, the world reveals itself to be a complex, swirling tapestry of tiny miracles.

Consider the complexity of language. We take a chaotic jumble of sounds and symbols, arrange them into sequences, and suddenly, we are sharing the contents of our souls. I can describe the smell of rain on hot asphalt, or the specific, melancholic ache of a half-forgotten memory, and you—miles or years away—can reach out and touch that exact feeling. It is a form of telepathy that we have turned into something mundane.

Perhaps that is the great tragedy of being human: we become desensitized to the magic of our own existence. We get so used to breathing that we forget the miracle of the lungs expanding; we get so used to thinking that we forget the wonder of the electric spark firing across a synapse. If we could only view the world with the wide-eyed curiosity of an observer seeing it for the very first time, we might find that every moment is, in its own quiet way, profoundly miraculous.

There is a peculiar beauty in the way the morning light catches the dust motes dancing in a sunbeam. Most of the time, we move through our days with blinkered vision, focused entirely on the destination—the next meeting, the unfinished task, the lingering to-do list. We treat our lives like a commute, something to be endured until we finally arrive at a point of rest. But if you stop for even a second, the world reveals itself to be a complex, swirling tapestry of tiny miracles.

Consider the complexity of language. We take a chaotic jumble of sounds and symbols, arrange them into sequences, and suddenly, we are sharing the contents of our souls. I can describe the smell of rain on hot asphalt, or the specific, melancholic ache of a half-forgotten memory, and you—miles or years away—can reach out and touch that exact feeling. It is a form of telepathy that we have turned into something mundane.

Perhaps that is the great tragedy of being human: we become desensitized to the magic of our own existence. We get so used to breathing that we forget the miracle of the lungs expanding; we get so used to thinking that we forget the wonder of the electric spark firing across a synapse. If we could only view the world with the wide-eyed curiosity of an observer seeing it for the very first time, we might find that every moment is, in its own quiet way, profoundly miraculous.
